metadata.title: Watch int8 Rounding Mash Your Activations

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Watching one shared scale keep the wide weight tensor crisp while mashing the narrow activation sine onto two levels finally showed me quantization is a range problem, not a bit-count problem. I want to pin down exactly where sharing breaks. Help me: (1) sweep the activation's range from 0.01 down to 0.001 and plot how many distinct int8 levels survive; (2) compare one global scale versus per-channel scales on that same tensor; (3) report the range ratio at which per-tensor scaling stops being safe.

props.command: Watching one shared scale keep the wide weight tensor crisp while mashing the narrow activation sine onto two levels finally showed me quantization is a range problem, not a bit-count problem. I want to pin down exactly where sharing breaks. Help me: (1) sweep the activation's range from 0.01 down to 0.001 and plot how many distinct int8 levels survive; (2) compare one global scale versus per-channel scales on that same tensor; (3) report the range ratio at which per-tensor scaling stops being safe.
props.segment: Watch int8 Rounding Mash Your Activations

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: To fit a model on a microcontroller you quantize it — float32 down to int8, four times smaller, and faster. It's the move that makes edge AI possible. But the same eight bits that leave your weights untouched can quietly destroy an activation tensor, and the model ships broken with no error. The question this video answers: when does int8 mash the signal, and why.
beats.visual_intent: title card: int8 quantization as the enabling move (4x smaller) with a warning — harmless for weights, lethal for narrow activations

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude — write quantize_demo dot py, int8 quant with a scale and zero-point, and show what the rounding does to a weight tensor spanning minus one-and-a-half to one-and-a-half versus an activation tensor spanning point-zero-zero-one to point-zero-one.

props.command:
claude "write quantize_demo.py:
  int8 quant (scale, zero_point)
  weights in [-1.5, 1.5]
  vs activations in [0.001, 0.01]
  show what the rounding does to each"

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The mapping is one line — q equals round of x over scale, plus the zero-point, clipped to the int8 range. Scale is the range divided by two-fifty-five. The whole story is in that denominator: one scale, calibrated on the wide tensor, applied to the narrow one.

props.code:
# quantize_demo.py  --  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

def quantize(x, lo, hi):
    scale = (hi - lo) / 255            # <- the denominator
    zp    = round(-128 - lo / scale)
    q     = np.clip(np.round(x/scale)+zp, -128, 127)
    return scale * (q - zp)            # dequantized

# A: weights   in [-1.5, 1.5]  -> scale 0.0118  (fine)
# B: activations in [0.001,0.01] SAME scale -> collapse

beats.narration_text: Run it. On the left, the weights: the crimson reconstruction rides right on top of the original — two-hundred-forty-nine distinct levels, error you'd never notice. On the right, the same scale on the activation sine, and the wave flattens onto two levels. Same eight bits. One tensor is fine; the other is gone.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: weights curve with dequantized staircase (249 levels); activation sine collapsing to 2 bars

beats.narration_text: That's a bug, not a law. The fix is to stop sharing one scale. Iterate: claude — update quantize_demo dot py, give each tensor its own min and max, per-channel, and re-run the collapse metric.

props.command:
claude "update quantize_demo.py:
  -> per-channel scale
  -> each tensor its own min/max
  -> re-run and show collapse metric"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. The activation sine comes back — two-hundred-fifty-five levels, the curve recovered. Nothing about the tensor changed; only the scale it was measured against did.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: activation sine recovered per-channel — 255 levels

beats.narration_text: So the damage was never int8 itself. It was one scale, set by the widest tensor, crushing the small-magnitude range where the signal actually lived. Per-channel gives each tensor its own ruler, and the signal survives.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: one scale for all -> mash vs per-channel -> preserved

beats.narration_text: Your move: dump a real activation tensor from your own model and run it through this — see which layers live near the rounding floor. And when your deploy toolchain quantizes, check whether it picked per-channel. If it didn't, that's where your accuracy went.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run your own activation dump, 2) verify per-channel in your toolchain

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. So the damage was never int8 itself. It was one scale, set by the widest tensor, crushing the small-magnitude range where the signal actually lived. Per-channel gives each tensor its own ruler, and the signal survives. Your move: dump a real activation tensor from your own model and run it through this — see which layers live near the rounding floor. And when your deploy toolchain quantizes, check whether it picked per-channel. If it didn't, that's where your accuracy went.
